intro:
Philistia was the coastal plain of southwestern Canaan occupied by the Philistines, stretching from Joppa southward to Gaza and bounded by the Mediterranean on the west and the Shephelah foothills on the east. Its five principal cities — Gaza, Ashdod, Ashkelon, Gath, and Ekron — each had its own ruler (seren). The psalmists use Philistia as a symbol of former enemies now subordinated to Israel's God: "Over Philistia I shout in triumph" (Psalms 60:8; 108:9). The plain's rich agricultural land and strategic coastal position made it a zone of perpetual contest between Israel and its neighbors from the judges period through the monarchy.

From the patriarchs onward Philistia shadowed Israel. Abraham and Isaac made covenants with Abimelech of Gerar in the days of the early settlers of the coast (Genesis 21:32–34; Genesis 26:1). In the era of the judges the Philistines became Israel’s great oppressor, and the LORD raised up Samson against them, who broke their power even in his death (Judges 13:1; Judges 16:30). At the rout of Ebenezer they captured the ark of God and carried it to Ashdod into the house of Dagon — but the idol fell broken before it, and the LORD’s heavy hand drove the cities to send the ark home (1 Samuel 4:10–11; 1 Samuel 5:1–4).

Under the monarchy the conflict came to a head. Goliath of Gath defied the armies of Israel until the shepherd David felled him with a stone (1 Samuel 17:49). David later sheltered among the Philistines at Ziklag under Achish of Gath (1 Samuel 27:6), and once enthroned he subdued them and broke their dominion (2 Samuel 5:25; 2 Samuel 8:1). Later kings Uzziah and Hezekiah struck their cities (2 Chronicles 26:6; 2 Kings 18:8), and the prophets pronounced doom on Gaza, Ashkelon, Ashdod, and Ekron for their cruelty (Amos 1:6–8; Jeremiah 47:1; Zephaniah 2:4–7).

Yet Scripture does not leave Philistia only under judgment. Zechariah foresaw the day when even Ekron would be “like a clan in Judah,” the unclean removed and a remnant of the old enemy belonging to God (Zechariah 9:7), and the psalm of the nations names Philistia among those born again in Zion (Psalms 87:4). So the coastal foe that long warred against the people of God becomes a sign of the Gentiles gathered at last under David’s greater Son, the Messiah in whom the nations find their citizenship.

link_tables.title: Places

cells: Gaza
Southernmost of the five cities; where Samson pulled down Dagon’s temple (Judg 16:21)
Ashdod
City of Dagon’s temple, where the captured ark broke the idol (1 Sam 5:1–3)
Ashkelon
A Philistine city Samson raided; mourned in David’s lament (2 Sam 1:20)
Gath
Home of Goliath, and David’s refuge under Achish (1 Sam 17:4)
Ekron
Northern city whose god Baal-zebub Ahaziah consulted (2 Kgs 1:2)
Gerar
Coastal town of Abimelech, where Abraham and Isaac sojourned (Gen 26:1)
Ziklag
Town Achish gave David while he hid from Saul (1 Sam 27:6)
Aphek
Where the Philistines routed Israel and seized the ark (1 Sam 4:1)
Joppa
Port marking the northern reach of the Philistine coast (Josh 19:46)

link_tables.note: Click any name to open its article. Markers for these places and the event below appear on the map above.

cells: The Ark in Philistia
Captured to Ashdod, the ark cast down Dagon and plagued the cities until sent home (1 Sam 5:1–6)
Abimelech
King of Gerar who made covenant with Abraham (Gen 21:32)
Samson
Judge who fought the Philistines and died destroying Dagon’s temple (Judg 16:30)
Delilah
Philistine woman who betrayed Samson’s secret (Judg 16:18)
Goliath
Champion of Gath struck down by David (1 Sam 17:49)
Achish
King of Gath who sheltered the fugitive David (1 Sam 27:2–3)
David
Killed Goliath and finally subdued Philistia as king (2 Sam 8:1)
Samuel
Led Israel to victory at Ebenezer after the ark’s return (1 Sam 7:13)
Saul
Warred with the Philistines all his days and fell on Gilboa (1 Sam 31:1–2)
